content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance •• Administration •• Executive/Legal •• Enforcement •• Statement of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662     322-5660         322-3901        322-6441           322-6444

January 11, 1983


Raymond M. Haignt
City Attorney
4740 Scotts Valley Drive, Suite A
P.O. Box 66659
Scotts Valley, CA  95066

                                        Re: Your Request for Written
                                            Advice, Our File No.
                                            A-82-202

Dear Mr. Haignt:

     Thank you for your recent letter requesting written
advice on behalf of two of your councilmembers, Ray Carl
and Rod Pulley. Your letter concisely states the facts and
the question, as follows:

     The fact situation is as follows:

     1. The City Council is desirous of accomplishing
construction of certain public improvements, such as
sanitary sewers, storm drains, traffic signals, and the
like, in the Pasatiempo Pines area by means of an
assessment district.

     2. Two members of the City Council, Councilman
Ray Carl and Councilman Rod Pulley, own property within
the boundaries of the proposed assessment district.

     3. Councilman Carl owns one parcel of land
improved with a single-family residence, and Councilman
Pulley owns a 1-1/2 acre parcel which has an approved
tentative map for 2 additional lots. Each councilman
resides at his parcel.

     4. The boundaries of the assessment district
encompass approximately 300 parcels of land improved
with single-family residences. Approximately 1,000
people reside within the boundaries of the assessment
district.

Raymond M. Haight
January 11, 1983
Page 2

     5.  Scotts Valley has a population of approximately 7,000 people, and has approximately 2,700
dwelling units within its boundaries (the vast majority
of which are single-family residences).

     6.  The vast majority of the single-family
residences located within the boundaries of the
assessment district are owner occupied.  The vast
majority of dwelling units located in Scotts Valley are
also owner occupied.

     7.  The parcels owned by the two Councilmen would
be affected by the public improvements to be made
within the boundaries of the assessment district.  Like
everyone else within the boundaries of the assessment
district, their parcels would be assessed for such
things as sanitary sewer and storm drain work.  While I
do not as yet have an accurate figure of what the
proposed assessments might be, I believe it is safe to
assume that the assessments against the parcels would
undoubtedly amount to several thousand dollars.  In
addition, while I do not have a precise figure as to
the value of the improved parcels owned by the two
Councilmen, I believe it is safe to assume that the
value of their respective improved parcels is well in
excess of $100,000.  I believe it is also safe to
assume that the value of all other single-family
residences located within the boundaries of the
assessment district likewise well exceeds $100,000.

     The question presented to the Commission by you is as
follows:

          Do Councilman Carl and Pulley have a conflict of
     interest such as would prohibit them from participating
     in voting on the formation of the assessment district,
     and in voting on the levying of assessments?

ANALYSIS

     Under the Political Reform Act,1/ a public official
may not make, participate in making, or use his or her
official position to influence, a governmental decision if

_____________
     1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91014.  All
statutory references are to the Government Code unless
otherwise specified.

Raymond M. Height
January 11, 1983
Page 3


he or she knows or has reason to know that he or she has a
financial interest in the decision. Section 87100. A
financial interest is defined by Section 87103 as follows:

     An official has a financial interest in a decision
     within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
     foreseeable that the decision will have a material
     financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
     the public generally, on:

                              * * *

               (b) Any real property in which the public
          official has a direct or indirect investment worth
          more than one thousand dollars ($1,000).

     Clearly, Councilmembers Carl and Pulley are public
officials, each with an interest in his respective real
property in excess of $1,000. Just as clearly, the effect
of the decision will be material as to both councilmembers'
property. (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(2)(B) (copy
enclosed).) Therefore, the remaining question is whether
the effect on their properties is distinguishable from
the effect on the public generally. Section 87103. This term
is refined by our regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18703, which states in part:

     A material financial effect of a governmental
     decision on an official's interests, as described in
     Government Code Section 87103(a) through (d), is
     distinguishable from its effect on the public generally
     unless the decision will effect the official's interest
     in substantially the same manner as it will affect all
     members of the public or a significant segment of the
     public. Except as provided herein, an industry, trade
     or profession does not constitute a significant segment
     of the general public.

     With respect to the decision to form the district, both
Mr. Carl and Mr. Pulley may well have a conflict of
interest. The decision to grant this special treatment2/
to one part of Scotts Valley and not to others would most

_____________________

2/ This assumes that other areas within Scotts
Valley are not already organized into assessment districts
and that Pasatiempo Pines is being singled out.

Raymond M. Haight
January 11, 1983
Page 4


likely create a differential effect upon Mr. Carl's and
Mr. Pulley's properties when compared with properties in
other parts of town. Consequently, any material financial
effect upon their properties resulting from the decision to
form the assessment district would be distinguishable from
the decision's effect on the public generally. Once the
district is formed, there may still be potential conflicts.
Councilmember Pulley really has three lots, two of which
are not yet developed. Consequently, the facts may well be
that his interests will be affected in a manner which is
distinguishable from the other lot owners in the proposed
assessment district. Councilmember Pulley's financial
interests are similar to question (b) in the Commission's
Owen Opinion,3/ and also close to the situation in the
Oglesby Opinion,4/. As an owner of undeveloped properties, he may stand to reap additional benefits from the
improvements made by the assessment district. Therefore,
Councilmember Pulley may be required to disqualify himself
from participation in the decision to form the assessment
district and to levy assessments.

Councilmember Carl's financial interests, on the other
hand, will be affected in a manner which is very similar to
other landowners in the proposed assessment district.
Thus, as to decisions on assessments, once the district is
formed, he would appear to have a conflict of interest
requiring disqualification only if the Pasatiempo Pines
assessment district is being assessed differently than
other districts in the city over which he has assessment
authority.


_______________________

3/ 2 PPPC Opinions 77, No. 76-005, June 2, 1976
(copy enclosed). The situation in the Owen Opinion
involved creation of a "core-area" plan for the City of
Davis. While the Commission found no conflict for two
officials, one of whom owned a single-family residence near
the core area ("situation (a)") and the other of whom
rented his small shop space in the core area ("situation
(c)"), the Commission did find a conflict on the part of a
core area land owner whose property was being commercially
developed ("situation (b)").

4/ 1 FPPC Opinions 71, No. 75-083, July 2, 1975. In
the Oglesby Opinion, the official in question had three
parcels of property in the proposed redevelopment area.

Raymond M. Haight
January 11, 1983
Page 5


CONCLUSION

Both councilmembers should disqualify themselves as to
the decision to form the assessment district and any
assessment decisions which will affect the district in a
manner different than any other such districts in Scotts
Valley. Councilmember Pulley should continue to disqualify
himself on assessment and other decisions which will affect
his property differently than most other properties within
the district, if the district is formed.

Should you have any questions, please feel free to call
me at 916/322-5901.

Sincerely,

Robert E. Leidigh
Counsel
Legal Division

REL:km
Enclosures

RAYMOND M. HAIGHT
ATTORNEY AT LAW
4740 SCOTTS VALLEY DRIVE, SUITE A
P O BOX 66659
SCOTTS VALLEY CALIFORNIA 95066
(408) 438-6610

December 10, 1982

Robert Leidigh, Esq.
Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA. 95804

Re: Request for Informal Opinion
    Proposed Pasatiempo Pines Assessment District

Dear Mr. Leidigh:

The City Council of the City of Scotts Valley has requested
that I write you for the purpose of obtaining an informal
opinion regarding conflict of interest.

The fact situation is as follows:

1. The City Council is desirous of accomplishing con-
struction of certain public improvements, such as sanitary
sewers, storm drains, traffic signals, and the like, in the
Pasatiempo Pines area by means of an assessment district.

2. Two members of the City Council, Councilman Ray Carl
and Councilman Rod Pulley, own property within the boundaries
of the proposed assessment district.

3. Councilman Carl owns one parcel of land improved with
a single-family residence, and Councilman Pulley owns a 1½ acre
parcel improved with a single-family residence, which has an
approved tentative map for 2 additional lots. Each councilman
resides at his parcel.

4. The boundaries of the assessment district encompass
approximately 300 parcels of land improved with single-family
residences. Approximately 1,000 people reside within the
boundaries of the assessment district.

5. Scotts Valley has a population of approximately 7,000
people, and has approximately 2,700 dwelling units within its
boundaries (the vast majority of which are single-family
residences).

6. The vast majority of the single-family residences
located within the boundaries of the assessment district are
owner occupied. The vast majority of dwelling units located

Robert Leigh, Esq.
December 10, 1982
Page 2


in Scotts Valley are also owner occupied.

7. The parcels owned by the two Councilmen would be
affected by the public improvements to be made within the
boundaries of the assessment district. Like everyone else
within the boundaries of the assessment district, their
parcels would be assessed for such things as sanitary sewer
and storm drain work. While I do not as yet have an accurate
figure of what the proposed assessments might be, I believe
it is safe to assume that the assessments against the parcels
would undoubtedly amount to several thousand dollars. In
addition, while I do not have a precise figure as to the
value of the improved parcels owned by the two Councilmen,
I believe it is safe to assume that the value of their res-
pective improved parcels is well in excess of $100,000.00.
I believe it is also safe to assume that the value of all
other single-family residences located within the boundaries
of the assessment district likewise well exceeds $100,000.00.

The question presented is as follows: Do Councilman Carl and
Pulley have a conflict of interest such as would prohibit them
from participating in voting on the formation of the assessment
district, and in voting on the levying of assessments?

If you are in need of further information or have any questions
regarding this matter, please feel free to call me at any time.

Very truly yours,

[signature]

RAYMOND M. HAIGHT
City Attorney

RMH:bc

CC  Robert Rockett, City Administrator
    Rod Pulley, Councilman
    Ray Carl, Councilman

assessment decisions which will affect the district in a 
manner different than any other such districts in Scotts 
Valley. 
Councilmember Pulley should continue to disqualify 
himself on assessment and other decisions which will affect 
his property differently than most other properties within 
tne district, if the district is formed.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether two city councilmembers who own property within a proposed assessment district have a conflict of interest that would prohibit them from voting on the district's formation and assessment levies.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Both councilmembers must disqualify themselves from voting on district formation and decisions affecting the district differently than other districts. Councilmember Pulley must additionally disqualify himself from votes affecting his undeveloped properties differently than other properties in the district.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: This is FPPC File No. A-82-202 dated January 11, 1983 (responding to December 10, 1982 request). Document includes both the original request letter and FPPC's formal response with detailed legal analysis citing Government Code sections and prior FPPC opinions (Owen, Oglesby).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Do Councilman Carl and Pulley have a conflict of interest such as would prohibit them from participating in voting on the formation of the assessment district, and in voting on the levying of assessments?

CONCLUSION: Both councilmembers should disqualify themselves as to 
the decision to form the assessment district and any 
assessment decisions which will affect the district in a 
manner different than any other such districts in Scotts 
Valley. 
Councilmember Pulley should continue to disqualify 
himself on assessment and other decisions which will affect 
his property differently than most other properties within 
tne district, if the district is formed.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that two Scotts Valley councilmembers who own residential property in a proposed assessment district have conflicts of interest requiring disqualification from voting on the district's formation and related assessment decisions. The analysis distinguishes between Councilmember Carl's interest (similar to other homeowners) and Councilmember Pulley's interest in undeveloped lots that may benefit differentially from improvements.
